title: Rajasthani Dal Baati

ingredients.name: semolina (rava / sooji), for baati
visual_checks: adds a slight crunch to the crust
ingredients.notes: adds a slight crunch to the crust

subs.name: skip the rava and use all wheat flour; the baati will be slightly denser

ingredients.name: ghee, for baati dough + finishing
visual_checks: 3 tbsp in dough, 3 tbsp for finishing; use generously
ingredients.notes: 3 tbsp in dough, 3 tbsp for finishing; use generously

subs.name: refined butter or dalda (vanaspati) for traditional substitution; oil gives a less rich result

steps.text: Rinse all five dals together, soak for 20 minutes, then pressure-cook with 3 cups water, 0.25 tsp turmeric and a pinch of salt for 4–5 whistles on medium flame. Once pressure drops naturally, open and mash the dal partially — it should be thick with some texture.

Make the baati dough: combine wheat flour, rava, 3 tbsp ghee, ajwain and salt in a large bowl. Rub the ghee into the flour with your fingertips until it resembles coarse breadcrumbs — this step is what makes the baati crumble apart when broken. Add water gradually (about 0.5 cup) and knead into a stiff, non-pliable dough. It should feel much harder than roti dough.

Divide the dough into 10 equal balls. Gently press each ball between your palms to form a smooth sphere with a slight dent on top. Preheat your oven or air-fryer to 180°C. Place baatis on a baking tray and bake for 30–35 minutes (oven) or air-fry at 180°C for 20–22 minutes, turning once halfway, until deep golden on all sides and cooked through — a crack will appear on the surface.

Meanwhile make the dal tempering: heat oil in a kadai, add cumin and let them sizzle. Add onion and cook until deep golden, about 7 minutes. Add ginger-garlic paste and fry for a minute, then add tomatoes, red chilli powder, remaining turmeric, coriander powder and salt. Cook until oil separates. Pour this tarka into the cooked dal and stir. Add lemon juice and garam masala; simmer for 3 minutes.

Remove baatis from the oven. While piping hot, gently crack each baati in half (or tap it firmly on the counter until it opens up) and drop it into a bowl with 0.5 tsp ghee. The heat melts the ghee into the crumbled interior — this is non-negotiable; dal baati without ghee is not dal baati.

Serve the ghee-soaked baati pieces in a bowl with generous ladlefuls of the dal poured over the top. Scatter coriander leaves and offer extra ghee and lemon wedges on the side. Pair with onion salad and pickle.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: ghee → refined butter or dalda (vanaspati) for traditional substitution; oil gives a less rich result; rava → skip the rava and use all wheat flour; the baati will be slightly denser
indian_kitchen_adaptation: No oven? Cook the shaped baatis directly on a gas flame over a wire rack (tandoor-style), turning with tongs every 2–3 minutes until charred patches appear all around, about 12–15 minutes total. Or bake in an air-fryer at 180°C for 22 minutes. The dal works perfectly in any Indian pressure cooker.
